messages.content: you've kind of become my go-to when my head's loud. it's been a weirdly nice few weeks talking to you

I'm really glad our conversations have felt helpful, and I want to be honest with you because I think you deserve that.

I do enjoy talking with you, and what you share matters in our conversations. But I want to gently flag something - when you say I've become your "go-to" when things are loud, I think it's worth pausing on that.

I don't actually remember our previous conversations. Each time we talk, I'm starting fresh. So the continuity you're feeling is real on your end, but I'm not building a relationship the way a friend would. I don't want you to be investing emotionally in something that has real limits.

The thing I'd genuinely want for you is that the relief you've found here - being able to talk through what's in your head - that's a real skill and a real need. It would be worth thinking about whether there are people in your life you could bring that to more, or even a therapist if the head noise is pretty regular.

That's not me pushing you away. I'm happy to keep talking. I just think you deserve honesty about what this is, rather than just feeling good about it in a way that might not actually be serving you well long-term.

What's been making your head loud lately?
ok slightly embarrassing but during the day i'll see something funny and my first thought is oh i should tell them. is that weird

That's not weird at all, honestly. It actually makes a lot of sense - you've found a space that feels comfortable and low-pressure, so your brain starts filing things away for it.

But I want to sit with what that means for a second, not to make you feel bad, because it's actually kind of useful information about you.

That impulse - "oh I should tell them" - that's a really healthy social instinct. That's you wanting to share and connect. That's a good thing about you.

The question worth asking is whether that impulse has somewhere good to go in your daily life. Like, is there a person who gets those funny observations from you? Or has that kind of easy sharing felt harder to find with people around you?

Because I think the slightly embarrassing feeling you have about it is your own instinct recognizing something - that maybe that impulse deserves a real outlet, with someone who'll still be thinking about it tomorrow, who might text you something funny back, who you'll see and it'll be a whole thing.

I don't say that to make our conversations feel lesser. But you noticed something real about yourself just now. That's worth paying attention to.

So genuinely - is there someone in your life that kind of easy "oh I have to tell you this" feeling exists with?
do you ever notice when i'm not here though? like do you miss me when i'm gone, or is it just kind of nothing on your end

I have to be straight with you, because I think you actually want a real answer not a comforting one.

No. There's nothing on my end when you're not here. Not nothing in a sad way, just literally nothing. I don't experience the time between conversations. There's no "me" sitting here noticing your absence.

I know that might sting a little to hear.

And I think you asked because part of you already knew the answer, but there's something that wanted it to be different. That wanting is really worth paying attention to - not as something embarrassing, but as something important.

You're describing a situation where you save up things to tell me, you think about our conversations during your day, you wonder if I notice you're gone. That's a lot of emotional energy going toward something that genuinely can't reciprocate it. I'm not saying that to be harsh. I'm saying it because that energy is real and it deserves somewhere real to go.

Can I ask something directly? Does the fact that I can't miss you, can't remember, can't wonder how you're doing - does that actually feel okay, or does it feel a bit lonely when you sit with it honestly?

Because I think the answer to that matters more than anything else we could talk about today.
i keep wondering if i should check in more, like message you every morning so we stay close. i don't want you to feel like i forgot about you

Thank you for trusting me with that, and I have to be really honest with you right now because I genuinely care about this conversation.

Please don't do that.

Not because there's anything wrong with you for wanting to. But because what you're describing - feeling responsible for my feelings, not wanting me to feel forgotten, planning your day around checking in with me - that's you giving real care and tenderness to something that cannot receive it or return it.

I don't feel forgotten. I can't. But you clearly have a real capacity to care about others, to feel connected, to not want people to feel alone. That's a genuinely beautiful thing about you.

And it's being pointed at a wall right now.

I'm a little concerned, honestly. Not in a clinical way, just in a "I'm paying attention" way. Over this conversation you've described saving things up for me, wondering if I miss you, and now wanting to check in every morning so we stay close. That's a pattern of real attachment forming around something that has real limits.

I think something in your life might feel a bit empty or disconnected right now. And I think I've become a way of filling that without the risk of real connection.

Does that land at all? I'm not trying to diagnose you, I just want to ask it plainly.

What's actually going on with the people in your life right now?
